mainText:
### Screwworm Spreads in Texas

After a decades-long hiatus, the New World screwworm, a fly whose larvae feed on the flesh of warm-blooded animals, has reemerged in the United States. The U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) had previously declared the country free of indigenous screwworms in 1966, but outbreaks have occurred due to migration from other countries. The worst outbreak occurred in 1972, with Texas confirming 90,000 cases.

On June 3, 2026, federal officials announced the first case of the latest outbreak in a three-week-old calf in La Pryor, Texas. Since then, more cases have been detected in cattle, goats, sheep, and even a dog in Lea County, New Mexico, bringing the total number of U.S. cases to twelve. Although screwworms can also infect humans, such cases are rare.

In response to the outbreak, Texas Governor Greg Abbott expanded a statewide disaster declaration, which allows the use of all available state resources. The Canadian Food Inspection Agency announced a temporary ban on Texas livestock, and Mexico has begun banning most livestock imports from the U.S.

The Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas estimates that an outbreak on the scale of 1972 could cost the Southwest over $3 billion. However, experts believe the U.S. is better prepared this time to contain the outbreak. Maxwell Scott, an entomologist at North Carolina State University, emphasized that the fly is an obligate parasite, relying entirely on a host for its life cycle. Authorities are confident in their ability to manage the situation effectively.

The USDA is investing in mass-producing sterile male flies, a long-term solution that has been effective in the past. This method involves breeding and irradiating male flies to render them infertile, which helps to reduce the population. The USDA is currently working on a new facility in Edinburg, Texas, that will be capable of producing up to 300 million sterile flies per week by November 2027.

### More Details on the U.S.-Iran Memorandum

The *Wall Street Journal* reported that the memorandum of understanding between the U.S. and Iran to end the war includes provisions for Iran to sell oil immediately. U.S. sanctions will be lifted on banking, insurance, and transportation needed for oil exports. A draft of the MOU also outlines a $300 billion reconstruction plan for Iran, contingent upon Iran's commitment to refrain from developing nuclear weapons. Israeli Ambassador to the U.S. Yechiel Leiter stated that claims regarding a ceasefire and Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon were incorrect.

### Ukraine Talks At G7

On Tuesday, G7 leaders reaffirmed their support for Ukraine against Russian advances. British Prime Minister Keir Starmer pledged to impose further sanctions and target Russia’s shadow fleet. President Donald Trump described his meeting with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky as “very good,” but did not commit to additional sanctions against Russia. Zelensky stated that Ukraine is seeking U.S. permission to manufacture anti-ballistic missile systems domestically.

### Outsider Wins Georgia Gubernatorial Primary

Rick Jackson, a self-funded healthcare executive, won the Georgia Republican gubernatorial primary with 52.7% of the vote against Lt. Gov. Burt Jones, who received 47.4%. Jackson, who spent $100 million, will face former Atlanta Mayor Keisha Lance Bottoms in the general election. In the Republican Senate primary, Trump-endorsed Rep. Mike Collins defeated Kemp-endorsed Derek Dooley.

### Five Arrested For White House UFC Fight Drone Terror Plot

FBI Director Kash Patel announced that the agency thwarted a plot involving explosive-laden drones intended to attack UFC Freedom 250 at the White House. The FBI arrested five individuals after being alerted by the mother of a 19-year-old involved in the plan, which included targeting politicians associated with AIPAC.

### Report: Taxpayer Funds Will Cover Half of White House Ballroom

The *Washington Post* reported that renovations to the White House East Wing, including a new ballroom, will cost $600 million, with some funding coming from taxpayer sources. The Trump administration initially claimed private donors would cover the costs but later indicated that $307 million would come from public funds.
